title: Fee-Charging Rates of Carbon Fees
text: Taiwan announced a standard carbon fee rate of NT$300 per tCO2e effective January 1, 2025. A preferential rate of NT$50 per tCO2e applies to entities that submit and receive approval for carbon reduction action plans meeting specified benchmarks. A second preferential rate of NT$100 per tCO2e applies to companies that commit to Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) pathways. The carbon fee is levied by the Ministry of Environment on regulated entities. The fee-charging targets in the initial phase are power generation and manufacturing businesses whose annual greenhouse gas emissions exceed 25,000 tCO2e. Companies subject to the fee must report verified emissions and pay fees annually.
customer_impact: Large emitters face direct carbon cost exposure at NT$300/tCO2e. Companies in manufacturing supply chains need updated cost modelling and emissions tracking. Even non-regulated suppliers may receive customer questionnaires requiring Scope 3 data.

title: Carbon Fee Imposition for Dedicated Funds
text: Taiwan carbon fees collected from regulated entities are deposited into a dedicated Climate Change Fund administered by the Climate Change Administration. The fund is used to support emissions reduction projects, green energy transition, and climate adaptation measures. Initial scope targets power generation and manufacturing businesses emitting more than 25,000 tCO2e per year. The first carbon fee bills were issued in 2025 for the 2024 reporting year emissions. Companies must submit verified greenhouse gas inventory reports as the basis for fee calculation. The CCA maintains a registry of regulated entities and their annual fee obligations.
customer_impact: Manufacturing customers must identify whether their facilities exceed the 25,000 tCO2e threshold. Companies need to set up or improve GHG inventory systems. Businesses with multiple facilities should evaluate consolidated vs. facility-level reporting.

title: Adoption of IFRS Sustainability Disclosure Standards (S1/S2)
text: The Financial Supervisory Commission announced that Taiwan will adopt FSC-endorsed IFRS Sustainability Disclosure Standards (IFRS S1 and IFRS S2) in phases starting from fiscal year 2026 for TWSE and TPEx listed companies. IFRS S1 covers general sustainability-related financial disclosures. IFRS S2 specifically addresses climate-related disclosures including governance, strategy, risk management, and metrics and targets. Large listed companies will be required to disclose in accordance with the standards first, followed by smaller listed companies in subsequent years. Companies must disclose material sustainability risks, opportunities, and their financial impacts. The FSC indicated that assurance requirements will also be phased in.
customer_impact: All TWSE/TPEx listed companies need to begin IFRS S1/S2 gap analysis immediately. Companies must establish governance structures for sustainability oversight at board level. Climate scenario analysis, Scope 1-3 emissions disclosure, and financial impact quantification will be required.

title: All Listed Companies Complete 2024 Sustainability Report Submission
text: Taiwan Stock Exchange announced that all 1,029 listed companies met the August 31, 2025 deadline for submitting their 2024 sustainability reports. This marks the first year that all listed companies, including smaller-capitalisation companies, were required to submit sustainability reports. The reports cover environmental, social, and governance performance. TWSE noted improvements in report quality compared to previous years, particularly in climate-related disclosures. Companies are encouraged to align future reports with international frameworks including GRI, SASB, and the forthcoming IFRS S1/S2 requirements.
customer_impact: The 1,029-company reporting universe defines the addressable market for ESGRegWatch. ESG consulting firms face rising demand for regulatory monitoring, report preparation, and compliance verification services across this population.

title: Sustainable Development Action Plans for TWSE and TPEx Listed Companies
text: The FSC published a comprehensive sustainable development action plan roadmap for TWSE and TPEx listed companies covering the period 2023 to 2026. The roadmap specifies phased requirements for board-level ESG oversight, sustainability committee establishment, third-party assurance of sustainability reports, and adoption of international disclosure standards. Listed companies are categorised by market capitalisation into different compliance phases. The plan emphasises integration of ESG risks into corporate governance, risk management frameworks, and financial planning. Companies are required to disclose how sustainability risks and opportunities affect their business strategy and financial performance.
customer_impact: Listed companies need multi-year compliance roadmaps covering governance setup, assurance procurement, and disclosure upgrades. ESG consulting firms need to track which companies are in which compliance phase to prioritise client advisory services.

title: Greenhouse Gas Inventory Management Rules for Regulated Entities
text: The Ministry of Environment requires all regulated entities subject to the carbon fee to maintain verified greenhouse gas inventories in accordance with ISO 14064 or equivalent standards. Third-party verification bodies accredited by MOENV must conduct annual verification of GHG inventory reports. Regulated entities must submit verified reports by June 30 each year for the prior calendar year emissions. Penalties for late or inaccurate reporting can include fee surcharges and public disclosure of non-compliance. The GHG inventory must cover Scope 1 direct emissions from owned facilities. Scope 2 indirect emissions from purchased electricity must also be reported under the updated rules.
customer_impact: Companies need to contract accredited third-party verifiers annually. GHG inventory software or systems that produce ISO 14064-compliant reports are required. ESG managers must track the June 30 submission deadline and coordinate with operations teams for data collection.

title: Third-Party Assurance Requirements for Sustainability Reports
text: The FSC requires large TWSE-listed companies with paid-in capital above NT$10 billion to obtain limited assurance on key sustainability data in their annual sustainability reports starting from the 2025 reporting year. Assurance must be performed by qualified assurance providers meeting FSC-specified competency requirements. The assurance scope covers at minimum: Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG emissions, energy consumption, and water withdrawal data. Companies must disclose the assurance statement and the assurance provider's name in the sustainability report. Smaller listed companies will be required to obtain assurance in subsequent phases as the FSC expands the requirement.
customer_impact: Large listed companies must budget for assurance engagements (typically NT$500,000-2,000,000 per engagement). ESG managers need to prepare data collection protocols that satisfy assurance provider requirements. Companies must select qualified assurance providers from the FSC-approved list.

title: TWSE Enhanced Climate Disclosure Requirements from 2026
text: Taiwan Stock Exchange announced enhanced climate disclosure requirements aligned with IFRS S2 for listed companies starting from fiscal year 2026 annual reports. Companies must disclose climate-related risks and opportunities assessed across short, medium, and long-term time horizons. Physical climate risks (e.g., flooding, heat stress, water scarcity) and transition risks (e.g., carbon pricing, technology change, policy shifts) must both be evaluated. Companies in high-emission industries including steel, cement, petrochemicals, semiconductors, and electronics manufacturing face heightened scrutiny. Scenario analysis using at least two climate scenarios is recommended for large companies. The financial impact of material climate risks must be quantified and disclosed.
customer_impact: Listed companies need to conduct formal climate risk assessments in 2025 to meet the 2026 disclosure deadline. Companies in high-emission industries face heightened disclosure requirements. ESG teams need climate scenario analysis capabilities or external advisory support.
